title: Linked List - Intersection of Linked Lists

content.problemStatement:
You are given heads of 222 linked lists.

Find the point where these two linked lists **intersect** with each other and return the node data at the intersection.

Input:
First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains of four lines of input.
First line contains two space separated integers N,M,N, M,N,M, and XXX, length of linked list AAA, BBB, and length of linked list after Merge Point.
Second line contains A1,A2,…ANA_1, A_2, \ldots A_NA1​,A2​,…AN​, value of the linked list nodes starting from the head for the first linked list.
Third line contains B1,B2,…BMB_1, B_2, \ldots B_MB1​,B2​,…BM​, value of the linked list nodes starting from the head for the second linked list.
Fourth line contains C1,C2,…CXC_1, C_2, \ldots C_XC1​,C2​,…CX​, value of the linked list nodes after the merge point of two linked lists.
**Note:**


For Java language, you need to:
Complete the function in the submit solution tab:

`Node intersectPoint(Node head1, Node head2){...}
` \  


For C++ language, you need to:
Complete the function in the submit solution tab:

`Node* intersectPoint(Node* head1, Node* head2){...}
` \  


For Python language, you need to:
Complete the function in the submit solution tab:

`def intersectPoint(headA, headB):
`Output:The function you complete should return the required answer.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains three space‑separated integers N, M and X — the lengths of the first list, the second list and the common tail after the merge point.
- The second line contains N integers, the node values of the first list before the merge.
- The third line contains M integers, the node values of the second list before the merge.
- The fourth line contains X integers, the node values of the common tail (the part shared by both lists).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the data value of the node where the two linked lists intersect (i.e., the first value of the common tail).

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case the first element of the common tail (the fourth line) is the intersection node: 43, 20 and 11 respectively.
Only one node is shared; its value 9 is the answer.
The common tail starts with 11, so the intersection node data is 11.

solution.approach:
The two lists are built such that after their individual prefixes they share exactly the same X nodes. Therefore the intersection point is simply the first node of the common tail. For each test case we read the three lengths, discard the two prefix sequences (they are not needed for the answer) and read the X values of the common tail. The required output is the first value of this tail. This yields O(N+M+X) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
If one prefers a generic linked‑list solution, compute the lengths of both lists, advance the longer list by the length difference, then move both pointers together until they meet; the meeting node’s data is the answer. The result is identical because the first common node is the head of the tail.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + M + X) per test case – we read each value once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input storage.
